title: Electric heater types compared: which is cheapest to run?
description: Fan, oil-filled, halogen, infrared or convector: every plug-in heater costs the same per unit of heat. What actually changes your bill, with current UK rates.
blurb: Every plug-in heater makes heat at the same cost per unit. The savings come from matching the type to the job.
lede: Walk down the heater aisle in any UK shop and you will see the same claims repeated on every box: efficient, economical, low energy. Here is the truth the packaging never mentions. Every plug-in electric heater, from a £15 fan heater to a £300 designer infrared panel, converts electricity into heat at exactly the same rate. One kilowatt-hour in, one kilowatt-hour of heat out. So the question is not which heater is most efficient. It is which heater lets you buy the fewest kilowatt-hours while still feeling warm, and that depends on what you are heating: a person, a small room for an evening, or a whole house through winter.
short_answer: All plug-in electric heaters cost the same per unit of heat: 26.11p per kWh on the current price cap, so a 2 kW heater run flat out costs about 52p an hour. The cheapest in practice is the one that uses the fewest kWh for your situation: a low-wattage radiant or infrared heater for warming one person, or an oil-filled radiator with a good thermostat for keeping a room warm over several hours. The only electric heaters genuinely cheaper per unit of heat are air-to-air heat pumps, which deliver roughly three units of heat per unit of electricity, and storage heaters charged on a cheap overnight tariff.

sections.h2: Why every plug-in heater costs the same per unit of heat
sections.html:
A resistance heater has one job: pass current through an element and turn it into heat. Physics guarantees that essentially 100 per cent of the electricity becomes heat in the room. A ceramic element, a halogen bulb, an oil-filled column and a bare wire coil all score identically. There is no clever technology inside a £200 heater that squeezes more warmth out of a kilowatt-hour than a £15 one.

This is why portable electric heaters carry no A to G efficiency rating. The familiar coloured arrows you see on fridges and washing machines, explained in our guide to energy labels, only make sense when products differ in how much energy they need for the same result. Resistance heaters cannot differ, so the label would read the same for all of them.

What does differ is power rating, and that sets the cost per hour. The sum is the same one we use across our appliance running cost guides: kilowatts multiplied by hours multiplied by your unit rate. A 2 kW heater running for one hour uses 2 kWh, which at 26.11p per kWh costs 52p. A 500 W panel running for the same hour uses 0.5 kWh and costs 13p, but it also delivers a quarter of the heat. Per unit of warmth, you paid exactly the same.

When a manufacturer calls a heater economical, they usually mean it has a lower wattage, a better thermostat, or a more targeted way of delivering heat. All three can save you money. None of them changes the price of a kilowatt-hour.

sections.h2: Running costs by heater type at current rates
sections.html:
The table below shows typical power ratings and what each type costs per hour. The final column matters most: once a room is up to temperature, a heater with a decent thermostat cycles on and off rather than drawing full power continuously, so the realistic hourly cost in a reasonably insulated room is often 50 to 70 per cent of the headline figure.

Heater typeTypical powerCost per hour, flat outRealistic cost per hour, thermostat cycling
Fan heater2,000 W52p26p to 37p
Ceramic fan heater1,800 W47p24p to 33p
Convector or panel heater2,000 W52p26p to 37p
Oil-filled radiator2,000 W52p26p to 37p
Halogen or radiant bar heater1,200 W31p31p (usually run continuously)
Infrared panel700 W18p13p to 18p
Electric fire, flame effect2,000 W52p26p to 37p
Air-to-air heat pump, heating mode800 W input for ~2,500 W of heat21p10p to 21p

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Thermostat figures assume a 50 to 70 per cent duty cycle once the room is warm; a draughty room will run closer to the flat-out figure.

One row stands out. The air-to-air heat pump, which is simply an air conditioning unit running in reverse, delivers around 2.5 kW of heat for 800 W of electricity. That works out near 8p per kWh of heat rather than 26p, because it moves heat from outside instead of generating it. No resistance heater can match that.

sections.h2: Radiant or convected heat: match the heater to the job
sections.html:
Since the price per kWh is fixed, the savings come from how the heat is delivered. Electric heaters split into two families, and picking the wrong one for your situation is where money quietly disappears.

Convection heaters warm the air

  • Fan heaters heat air fast and blow it around the room. Ideal for a quick blast in a bathroom or to take the edge off a cold home office while you wait for the room to warm. Run one all evening, though, and you are paying top dollar for noisy heat with no thermal mass behind it.
  • Convector and panel heaters do the same job silently and a little more gently. Fine for medium sessions in a small, well-sealed room.
  • Oil-filled radiators are the marathon runners. They take 15 to 20 minutes to get going, but the oil holds heat, so the element cycles off for long stretches once the room is warm. Over a three or four hour evening they typically use fewer kWh than a fan heater holding the same temperature, purely because the thermostat does more of the work.

Radiant heaters warm you

  • Halogen and radiant bar heaters throw infrared straight at whatever is in front of them. The air stays cold, but you feel warm within seconds. In a garage, a workshop, a conservatory or any space that is hopeless to heat, a 1,200 W halogen pointed at your chair beats a 2 kW fan heater trying and failing to warm the whole volume.
  • Infrared panels are the wall-mounted version: slim, silent and usually 350 W to 900 W. Because they heat surfaces and people rather than air, a well-placed panel can keep one person comfortable for under 20p an hour.

The rule of thumb: heating a person is cheap, heating air is expensive, and heating air in a leaky room is the most expensive of all. If you only need yourself warm, go radiant and low wattage. If you need the room itself warm for hours, go oil-filled with a proper thermostat.

sections.h2: The two exceptions that genuinely cost less per unit of heat
sections.html:
Two kinds of electric heating break the 26.11p rule, each in a different way.

Air-to-air heat pumps sidestep resistance heating altogether. A typical unit delivers around three units of heat per unit of electricity across a UK winter, which brings the effective cost down to roughly 8 to 10p per kWh of heat. That is in the same territory as mains gas. The catch is the up-front cost: a single-room split system professionally installed usually runs £1,500 to £3,000, so it only makes sense for a room you heat a lot, and it doubles as cooling in summer.

Storage heaters use ordinary resistance elements, but they buy their electricity at night on a time-of-use tariff and release the heat the next day. On an Economy 7 deal the overnight rate is typically well below the standard unit rate, often around half, which can roughly halve the cost per kWh of heat compared with a heater running on a flat daytime rate. The trade-offs are real: day-rate electricity on these tariffs is usually pricier, and the heat arrives on the bricks' schedule rather than yours. Our guide to storage heaters covers how to set the input and output dials so the charge actually lasts the evening.

One thing that is not an exception: gas. At 7.33p per kWh and roughly 90 per cent boiler efficiency, mains gas delivers heat at around 8p per kWh, about a third of the cost of any daytime resistance heater. If you have a gas boiler, the comparison worth doing is not heater versus heater but heater versus boiler, and we run those numbers in gas versus electric heating cost. The short version: one small electric heater in one occupied room can undercut firing the whole central heating system, but electric heating for the whole house almost never wins on a standard tariff.

sections.h2: How to cut the bill whichever heater you own
sections.html:
You cannot make the kilowatt-hours cheaper, but you can buy fewer of them.

  • Insist on a real thermostat. The single biggest difference between cheap and dear running is whether the heater cycles off. A heater with only a power dial and no temperature control runs flat out until you remember to switch it off. If yours has no thermostat, a £10 plug-in thermostat between the heater and the socket pays for itself in days.
  • Use the timer. Set the heater to come on 20 minutes before you use the room and off when you leave. An hour of forgotten 2 kW heating costs 52p, and forgotten hours add up fast.
  • Shrink the space. Shut the door, and if the room is large, heat the corner you occupy with a radiant heater rather than the whole volume. Draught-proofing the door and windows costs a few pounds and cuts the heater's duty cycle for good.
  • Set 18 to 19°C, not 23. Each extra degree adds roughly 10 per cent to the heat demand of the room.
  • Mind the standby draw. Heaters with remote controls and LED displays keep drawing a watt or two around the clock when left plugged in. It is pennies per heater, but the same habit across the house adds up, as our standby power guide shows. Switch off at the wall out of season.
  • Mind the safety basics too: keep heaters off extension leads, give them a metre of clearance, and never dry clothes directly on them.

There is no secretly efficient electric heater. At the current cap every plug-in type delivers heat at 26.11p per kWh, so a 2 kW heater costs about 52p an hour at full power and a fair bit less once a thermostat starts cycling. Choose by job, not by marketing: a 1,200 W halogen or a small infrared panel to keep one person warm in a cold space, an oil-filled radiator with a decent thermostat to hold a room at temperature through an evening, and a fan heater only for short blasts. If one room needs serious heating year after year, an air-to-air heat pump is the only electric option that genuinely cuts the price per unit of heat, by roughly two thirds. And if you have mains gas, the boiler delivers the same warmth at around a third of the price, so the electric heater's role is precision: one person, one room, one evening, switched off the moment it is not needed.

faq.q: Are oil-filled radiators cheaper to run than fan heaters?
faq.a: Not per unit of heat: both cost 26.11p per kWh at the current cap. But over a long session an oil-filled radiator usually uses fewer kWh, because its thermal mass and thermostat let the element switch off for long stretches once the room is warm. For a quick ten-minute blast, the fan heater wins; for a whole evening, the oil-filled radiator usually does.

faq.q: How much does it cost to run a 2 kW heater for an hour?
faq.a: About 52p at full power, based on 26.11p per kWh under the Ofgem price cap for July to September 2026. With a thermostat cycling in a reasonably sealed room, expect roughly 26p to 37p per hour once the room is up to temperature.

faq.q: What is the cheapest electric heater to run overnight?
faq.a: For warming a sleeping person, an electric blanket at around 100 W costs roughly 3p an hour, far cheaper than heating the bedroom air. If you must heat the room itself, a low-wattage oil-filled radiator with its thermostat set to about 16 to 18°C is the most controllable option. Storage heaters charged on an overnight tariff are cheaper still per unit, but they are a fixed installation rather than a plug-in.

faq.q: Are infrared panels more efficient than convector heaters?
faq.a: They convert electricity to heat at the same 100 per cent rate, so the unit cost is identical. The saving comes from how the heat is used: an infrared panel warms people and surfaces directly, so a 700 W panel can keep someone comfortable in a room where a convector would need 2 kW to warm all the air. Right panel, right position, fewer kilowatt-hours.

faq.q: Is an electric heater cheaper than putting the gas central heating on?
faq.a: Per unit of heat, gas wins easily: around 8p per kWh through a typical boiler against 26.11p for electricity. But the boiler heats the whole system. If you only need one small room warm for a couple of hours, a single 1 kW to 2 kW electric heater can cost less than firing every radiator in the house. The crossover depends on your home's size and insulation, so time both for an evening and compare meter readings.
